vulnerabilities.title: Command Injection via os.system()
vulnerabilities.description: Direct command execution using os.system() detected. This allows execution of arbitrary shell commands and can lead to remote code execution if user input is not properly sanitized.
vulnerabilities.remediation:
1. Avoid os.system() for executing commands
2. Use subprocess.run() with shell=False and list arguments
3. Validate and sanitize all user inputs
4. Use shlex.quote() for shell argument escaping if shell is necessary
5. Consider using safer alternatives like pathlib for file operations

vulnerabilities.title: Command Injection via os.system()

vulnerabilities.remediation:
1. Avoid os.system() for executing commands

3. Validate and sanitize all user inputs
4. Use shlex.quote() for shell argument escaping if shell is necessary
5. Consider using safer alternatives like pathlib for file operations

vulnerabilities.title: Command Injection via subprocess.call(shell=True)
vulnerabilities.description: subprocess.call() with shell=True detected. Using shell=True passes commands through the shell, enabling command injection if user input is included.
vulnerabilities.remediation:
1. Use shell=False (default) with subprocess.call()
2. Pass command as a list of arguments instead of string
3. Example: subprocess.call(['ls', '-l']) instead of subprocess.call('ls -l', shell=True)
4. If shell features are needed, use shlex.quote() for argument escaping

vulnerabilities.title: Command Injection via subprocess.call(shell=True)
vulnerabilities.description: subprocess.call() with shell=True detected. Using shell=True passes commands through the shell, enabling command injection if user input is included.
vulnerabilities.remediation:
1. Use shell=False (default) with subprocess.call()
2. Pass command as a list of arguments instead of string
3. Example: subprocess.call(['ls', '-l']) instead of subprocess.call('ls -l', shell=True)
4. If shell features are needed, use shlex.quote() for argument escaping

vulnerabilities.title: Command Injection via subprocess.run(shell=True)
vulnerabilities.description: subprocess.run() with shell=True detected. Shell interpretation of commands enables injection attacks when user-controlled data is included.
vulnerabilities.remediation:
1. Use shell=False (default) with subprocess.run()
2. Pass command as a list: subprocess.run(['command', 'arg1', 'arg2'])
3. Validate all inputs before including in commands
4. Use shlex.quote() if shell=True is absolutely necessary

vulnerabilities.title: Command Injection via subprocess.run(shell=True)
vulnerabilities.description: subprocess.run() with shell=True detected. Shell interpretation of commands enables injection attacks when user-controlled data is included.
vulnerabilities.remediation:
1. Use shell=False (default) with subprocess.run()
2. Pass command as a list: subprocess.run(['command', 'arg1', 'arg2'])
3. Validate all inputs before including in commands
4. Use shlex.quote() if shell=True is absolutely necessary

vulnerabilities.title: Command Injection via subprocess.Popen(shell=True)
vulnerabilities.description: subprocess.Popen() with shell=True detected. This creates a shell subprocess that can be exploited for command injection.
vulnerabilities.remediation:
1. Use shell=False with subprocess.Popen()
2. Pass arguments as a list instead of shell command string
3. Avoid concatenating user input into commands
4. Use shlex.split() to safely parse shell commands if needed

vulnerabilities.title: Code Injection via eval()
vulnerabilities.description: Usage of eval() detected. The eval() function executes arbitrary Python code and is extremely dangerous when used with user input. It can lead to remote code execution and complete system compromise.
vulnerabilities.remediation:
1. NEVER use eval() with user-controlled input
2. Use ast.literal_eval() for safe evaluation of literals
3. Use json.loads() for parsing JSON data
4. Consider using safer alternatives like configparser or yaml.safe_load()
5. If dynamic code execution is absolutely necessary, use sandboxing

4. Consider using safer alternatives like configparser or yaml.safe_load()
5. If dynamic code execution is absolutely necessary, use sandboxing

vulnerabilities.title: Code Injection via exec()
vulnerabilities.description: Usage of exec() detected. The exec() function executes arbitrary Python code and poses severe security risks when used with untrusted input. Can lead to complete system compromise.
vulnerabilities.remediation:
1. Avoid exec() entirely - there's almost always a better solution
2. NEVER use exec() with user-provided input
3. Refactor code to use proper function calls or imports
4. If dynamic behavior is needed, use plugin architectures with importlib
5. Consider using __import__() with strict module name validation

vulnerabilities.title: Code Injection via exec()

5. Consider using __import__() with strict module name validation

vulnerabilities.title: Path Traversal: Unsafe File Operation
vulnerabilities.description: Detected unsafe file operation: 'open("wget " + url, shell=True)'. File operations using concatenated or user-controlled paths are vulnerable to directory traversal attacks. Attackers can manipulate paths to read, write, or delete arbitrary files on the system.
vulnerabilities.remediation:
1. Avoid string concatenation for file paths
2. Use safe path joining with validation
3. Implement allowlist of permitted file operations
4. Validate file extensions and types
5. Use absolute paths with validation
6. Implement file access logging
7. Apply principle of least privilege

vulnerabilities.title: SQL Injection via Django raw()
vulnerabilities.description: Django User.objects.raw() used with f-string. This allows direct SQL injection if user input is included.
vulnerabilities.code_snippet: users = User.objects.raw(f"SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = {user_id}")
vulnerabilities.remediation:
1. Use Django ORM methods (filter, get) instead of raw SQL
2. If raw SQL is needed, use parameterized queries: raw('sql %s', [params])
3. NEVER use f-strings or format() to build SQL queries

vulnerabilities.title: XSS: Unescaped Template Rendering
vulnerabilities.description:
Detected unescaped template rendering: 'mark_safe(f"
{user_input}
")'. Using '|safe', 'mark_safe', or similar template filters disables automatic HTML escaping. This is dangerous when rendering user-provided content. Template engines like Jinja2 and Django automatically escape variables by default for security. Marking content as 'safe' removes this protection and can lead to stored XSS if the content comes from user input or untrusted sources.
vulnerabilities.remediation:
1. Always sanitize and validate user input before rendering
2. Use framework-provided auto-escaping (enabled by default in most frameworks)
3. Avoid innerHTML, outerHTML, and document.write with user input
4. Use .textContent or .innerText instead of .innerHTML for text
5. Implement Content Security Policy (CSP) headers
6. Use DOMPurify or similar library if HTML rendering is required
7. Never use javascript:, data:, or vbscript: protocols with user input
8. Prefer safe framework methods: .text() in jQuery, default rendering in React

vulnerabilities.title: Config: Debug Mode Enabled
vulnerabilities.description: Detected debug mode enabled: 'reload=True'. Running applications in debug mode in production exposes sensitive information including stack traces, environment variables, and internal application structure. Attackers can use this information to identify vulnerabilities and plan attacks. This is critical for MCP servers which may handle sensitive user data.
vulnerabilities.remediation:
1. Disable debug mode in production environments
2. Use environment variables to control debug settings
3. Set DEBUG=False or debug: false in production configs
4. Use separate configuration files for dev and production
5. Implement proper logging without exposing internals
6. Configure error pages that don't leak information
7. Review deployment checklist before production releases

vulnerabilities.title: Config: Weak Session/Secret Configuration
vulnerabilities.description: Detected weak secret configuration: 'config['SECRET_KEY'] = 'dev''. Weak or default secret keys allow attackers to forge sessions, decrypt data, and bypass authentication. Insecure cookie settings enable session theft.
vulnerabilities.remediation:
1. Use strong, randomly generated secret keys (32+ bytes)
2. Store secrets in environment variables, not code
3. Set SESSION_COOKIE_SECURE=True (HTTPS only)
4. Set SESSION_COOKIE_HTTPONLY=True (prevent XSS)
5. Set SESSION_COOKIE_SAMESITE='Lax' or 'Strict'
6. Rotate secrets regularly
7. Use secret management tools (Vault, AWS Secrets Manager)

vulnerabilities.title: Hardcoded AWS Access Key
vulnerabilities.description: AWS Access Key found: AKIA1A2B...********. This grants programmatic access to AWS resources.
vulnerabilities.remediation:
1. Remove the hardcoded secret from source code
2. Use environment variables or a secrets manager
3. Rotate the exposed credential
4. Implement secret scanning in CI/CD pipeline

vulnerabilities.description: OpenAI API Key found: sk-12345...********. This provides access to OpenAI's API services.

4. Rotate all credentials that may have been exposed

vulnerabilities.title: XSS: DOM-Based (innerHTML Manipulation)
vulnerabilities.description: Detected dangerous DOM manipulation: '.innerHTML = name'. Direct manipulation of innerHTML, outerHTML, or document.write with unsanitized input can lead to XSS attacks. Attackers can inject malicious scripts that execute in the user's browser context, potentially stealing cookies, session tokens, or performing actions on behalf of the user. This is especially critical in MCP web UIs where user-provided tool descriptions or results might be rendered.
vulnerabilities.remediation:
1. Always sanitize and validate user input before rendering
2. Use framework-provided auto-escaping (enabled by default in most frameworks)
3. Avoid innerHTML, outerHTML, and document.write with user input
4. Use .textContent or .innerText instead of .innerHTML for text
5. Implement Content Security Policy (CSP) headers
6. Use DOMPurify or similar library if HTML rendering is required
7. Never use javascript:, data:, or vbscript: protocols with user input
8. Prefer safe framework methods: .text() in jQuery, default rendering in React

vulnerabilities.title: Config: Debug Mode Enabled
vulnerabilities.description: Detected debug mode enabled: 'debug=True'. Running applications in debug mode in production exposes sensitive information including stack traces, environment variables, and internal application structure. Attackers can use this information to identify vulnerabilities and plan attacks. This is critical for MCP servers which may handle sensitive user data.
vulnerabilities.remediation:
1. Disable debug mode in production environments
2. Use environment variables to control debug settings
3. Set DEBUG=False or debug: false in production configs
4. Use separate configuration files for dev and production
5. Implement proper logging without exposing internals
6. Configure error pages that don't leak information
7. Review deployment checklist before production releases

3. Use environment variables or a secrets manager (e.g., HashiCorp Vault, AWS Secrets Manager)
4. Rotate all credentials that may have been exposed
5. Review access logs for unauthorized usage

vulnerabilities.title: Command Injection via os.system()
vulnerabilities.description: Direct command execution using os.system() detected. This allows execution of arbitrary shell commands and can lead to remote code execution if user input is not properly sanitized.
vulnerabilities.remediation:
1. Avoid os.system() for executing commands
2. Use subprocess.run() with shell=False and list arguments
3. Validate and sanitize all user inputs
4. Use shlex.quote() for shell argument escaping if shell is necessary
5. Consider using safer alternatives like pathlib for file operations

vulnerabilities.title: Command Injection via subprocess.call(shell=True)
vulnerabilities.description: subprocess.call() with shell=True detected. Using shell=True passes commands through the shell, enabling command injection if user input is included.
vulnerabilities.remediation:
1. Use shell=False (default) with subprocess.call()
2. Pass command as a list of arguments instead of string
3. Example: subprocess.call(['ls', '-l']) instead of subprocess.call('ls -l', shell=True)
4. If shell features are needed, use shlex.quote() for argument escaping

vulnerabilities.title: XSS: DOM-Based (innerHTML Manipulation)
vulnerabilities.description: Detected dangerous DOM manipulation: '.innerHTML = name'. Direct manipulation of innerHTML, outerHTML, or document.write with unsanitized input can lead to XSS attacks. Attackers can inject malicious scripts that execute in the user's browser context, potentially stealing cookies, session tokens, or performing actions on behalf of the user. This is especially critical in MCP web UIs where user-provided tool descriptions or results might be rendered.
vulnerabilities.remediation:
1. Always sanitize and validate user input before rendering
2. Use framework-provided auto-escaping (enabled by default in most frameworks)
3. Avoid innerHTML, outerHTML, and document.write with user input
4. Use .textContent or .innerText instead of .innerHTML for text
5. Implement Content Security Policy (CSP) headers
6. Use DOMPurify or similar library if HTML rendering is required
7. Never use javascript:, data:, or vbscript: protocols with user input
8. Prefer safe framework methods: .text() in jQuery, default rendering in React

vulnerabilities.title: SQL Injection via Django raw()
vulnerabilities.description: Django User.objects.raw() used with f-string. This allows direct SQL injection if user input is included.
vulnerabilities.code_snippet: users = User.objects.raw(f"SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = {user_id}")
vulnerabilities.remediation:
1. Use Django ORM methods (filter, get) instead of raw SQL
2. If raw SQL is needed, use parameterized queries: raw('sql %s', [params])
3. NEVER use f-strings or format() to build SQL queries

vulnerabilities.title: XSS: Unescaped Template Rendering
vulnerabilities.description:
Detected unescaped template rendering: 'mark_safe(f"
{user_input}
")'. Using '|safe', 'mark_safe', or similar template filters disables automatic HTML escaping. This is dangerous when rendering user-provided content. Template engines like Jinja2 and Django automatically escape variables by default for security. Marking content as 'safe' removes this protection and can lead to stored XSS if the content comes from user input or untrusted sources.
vulnerabilities.remediation:
1. Always sanitize and validate user input before rendering
2. Use framework-provided auto-escaping (enabled by default in most frameworks)
3. Avoid innerHTML, outerHTML, and document.write with user input
4. Use .textContent or .innerText instead of .innerHTML for text
5. Implement Content Security Policy (CSP) headers
6. Use DOMPurify or similar library if HTML rendering is required
7. Never use javascript:, data:, or vbscript: protocols with user input
8. Prefer safe framework methods: .text() in jQuery, default rendering in React

vulnerabilities.title: Config: Debug Mode Enabled
vulnerabilities.description: Detected debug mode enabled: 'reload=True'. Running applications in debug mode in production exposes sensitive information including stack traces, environment variables, and internal application structure. Attackers can use this information to identify vulnerabilities and plan attacks. This is critical for MCP servers which may handle sensitive user data.
vulnerabilities.remediation:
1. Disable debug mode in production environments
2. Use environment variables to control debug settings
3. Set DEBUG=False or debug: false in production configs
4. Use separate configuration files for dev and production
5. Implement proper logging without exposing internals
6. Configure error pages that don't leak information
7. Review deployment checklist before production releases

vulnerabilities.title: Config: Weak Session/Secret Configuration
vulnerabilities.description: Detected weak secret configuration: 'config['SECRET_KEY'] = 'dev''. Weak or default secret keys allow attackers to forge sessions, decrypt data, and bypass authentication. Insecure cookie settings enable session theft.
vulnerabilities.remediation:
1. Use strong, randomly generated secret keys (32+ bytes)
2. Store secrets in environment variables, not code
3. Set SESSION_COOKIE_SECURE=True (HTTPS only)
4. Set SESSION_COOKIE_HTTPONLY=True (prevent XSS)
5. Set SESSION_COOKIE_SAMESITE='Lax' or 'Strict'
6. Rotate secrets regularly
7. Use secret management tools (Vault, AWS Secrets Manager)

vulnerabilities.title: Hardcoded Generic API Key

vulnerabilities.remediation:
1. Immediately revoke this API key/token

3. Use environment variables or a secrets manager (e.g., HashiCorp Vault, AWS Secrets Manager)

vulnerabilities.title: Command Injection via os.system()
vulnerabilities.description: Direct command execution using os.system() detected. This allows execution of arbitrary shell commands and can lead to remote code execution if user input is not properly sanitized.
vulnerabilities.remediation:
1. Avoid os.system() for executing commands
2. Use subprocess.run() with shell=False and list arguments
3. Validate and sanitize all user inputs
4. Use shlex.quote() for shell argument escaping if shell is necessary
5. Consider using safer alternatives like pathlib for file operations

vulnerabilities.title: Command Injection via subprocess.call(shell=True)
vulnerabilities.description: subprocess.call() with shell=True detected. Using shell=True passes commands through the shell, enabling command injection if user input is included.
vulnerabilities.remediation:
1. Use shell=False (default) with subprocess.call()
2. Pass command as a list of arguments instead of string
3. Example: subprocess.call(['ls', '-l']) instead of subprocess.call('ls -l', shell=True)
4. If shell features are needed, use shlex.quote() for argument escaping

vulnerabilities.title: XSS: DOM-Based (innerHTML Manipulation)
vulnerabilities.description: Detected dangerous DOM manipulation: '.innerHTML = name'. Direct manipulation of innerHTML, outerHTML, or document.write with unsanitized input can lead to XSS attacks. Attackers can inject malicious scripts that execute in the user's browser context, potentially stealing cookies, session tokens, or performing actions on behalf of the user. This is especially critical in MCP web UIs where user-provided tool descriptions or results might be rendered.
vulnerabilities.remediation:
1. Always sanitize and validate user input before rendering
2. Use framework-provided auto-escaping (enabled by default in most frameworks)
3. Avoid innerHTML, outerHTML, and document.write with user input
4. Use .textContent or .innerText instead of .innerHTML for text
5. Implement Content Security Policy (CSP) headers
6. Use DOMPurify or similar library if HTML rendering is required
7. Never use javascript:, data:, or vbscript: protocols with user input
8. Prefer safe framework methods: .text() in jQuery, default rendering in React

vulnerabilities.title: SQL Injection via Django raw()
vulnerabilities.description: Django User.objects.raw() used with f-string. This allows direct SQL injection if user input is included.
vulnerabilities.code_snippet: users = User.objects.raw(f"SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = {user_id}")
vulnerabilities.remediation:
1. Use Django ORM methods (filter, get) instead of raw SQL
2. If raw SQL is needed, use parameterized queries: raw('sql %s', [params])
3. NEVER use f-strings or format() to build SQL queries

vulnerabilities.title: XSS: Unescaped Template Rendering
vulnerabilities.description:
Detected unescaped template rendering: 'mark_safe(f"
{user_input}
")'. Using '|safe', 'mark_safe', or similar template filters disables automatic HTML escaping. This is dangerous when rendering user-provided content. Template engines like Jinja2 and Django automatically escape variables by default for security. Marking content as 'safe' removes this protection and can lead to stored XSS if the content comes from user input or untrusted sources.
vulnerabilities.remediation:
1. Always sanitize and validate user input before rendering
2. Use framework-provided auto-escaping (enabled by default in most frameworks)
3. Avoid innerHTML, outerHTML, and document.write with user input
4. Use .textContent or .innerText instead of .innerHTML for text
5. Implement Content Security Policy (CSP) headers
6. Use DOMPurify or similar library if HTML rendering is required
7. Never use javascript:, data:, or vbscript: protocols with user input
8. Prefer safe framework methods: .text() in jQuery, default rendering in React

vulnerabilities.title: Config: Debug Mode Enabled
vulnerabilities.description: Detected debug mode enabled: 'reload=True'. Running applications in debug mode in production exposes sensitive information including stack traces, environment variables, and internal application structure. Attackers can use this information to identify vulnerabilities and plan attacks. This is critical for MCP servers which may handle sensitive user data.
vulnerabilities.remediation:
1. Disable debug mode in production environments
2. Use environment variables to control debug settings
3. Set DEBUG=False or debug: false in production configs
4. Use separate configuration files for dev and production
5. Implement proper logging without exposing internals
6. Configure error pages that don't leak information
7. Review deployment checklist before production releases

vulnerabilities.title: Config: Weak Session/Secret Configuration
vulnerabilities.description: Detected weak secret configuration: 'config['SECRET_KEY'] = 'dev''. Weak or default secret keys allow attackers to forge sessions, decrypt data, and bypass authentication. Insecure cookie settings enable session theft.
vulnerabilities.remediation:
1. Use strong, randomly generated secret keys (32+ bytes)
2. Store secrets in environment variables, not code
3. Set SESSION_COOKIE_SECURE=True (HTTPS only)
4. Set SESSION_COOKIE_HTTPONLY=True (prevent XSS)
5. Set SESSION_COOKIE_SAMESITE='Lax' or 'Strict'
6. Rotate secrets regularly
7. Use secret management tools (Vault, AWS Secrets Manager)

vulnerabilities.description: OpenAI API Key found: sk-12345...********. This provides access to OpenAI's API services.
vulnerabilities.remediation:
1. Immediately revoke this API key/token
2. Remove the hardcoded credential from the source code
3. Use environment variables or a secrets manager (e.g., HashiCorp Vault, AWS Secrets Manager)
4. Rotate all credentials that may have been exposed
5. Review access logs for unauthorized usage

vulnerabilities.title: Hardcoded Generic API Key
vulnerabilities.description: Generic API Key found: API_KEY ...********. Hardcoded secrets pose a security risk.
vulnerabilities.remediation:
1. Immediately revoke this API key/token
2. Remove the hardcoded credential from the source code
3. Use environment variables or a secrets manager (e.g., HashiCorp Vault, AWS Secrets Manager)

5. Review access logs for unauthorized usage

vulnerabilities.title: Command Injection via os.system()
vulnerabilities.description: Direct command execution using os.system() detected. This allows execution of arbitrary shell commands and can lead to remote code execution if user input is not properly sanitized.
vulnerabilities.remediation:
1. Avoid os.system() for executing commands
2. Use subprocess.run() with shell=False and list arguments
3. Validate and sanitize all user inputs
4. Use shlex.quote() for shell argument escaping if shell is necessary
5. Consider using safer alternatives like pathlib for file operations

vulnerabilities.title: Command Injection via subprocess.call(shell=True)
vulnerabilities.description: subprocess.call() with shell=True detected. Using shell=True passes commands through the shell, enabling command injection if user input is included.
vulnerabilities.remediation:
1. Use shell=False (default) with subprocess.call()
2. Pass command as a list of arguments instead of string
3. Example: subprocess.call(['ls', '-l']) instead of subprocess.call('ls -l', shell=True)
4. If shell features are needed, use shlex.quote() for argument escaping

vulnerabilities.title: XSS: DOM-Based (innerHTML Manipulation)
vulnerabilities.description: Detected dangerous DOM manipulation: '.innerHTML = name'. Direct manipulation of innerHTML, outerHTML, or document.write with unsanitized input can lead to XSS attacks. Attackers can inject malicious scripts that execute in the user's browser context, potentially stealing cookies, session tokens, or performing actions on behalf of the user. This is especially critical in MCP web UIs where user-provided tool descriptions or results might be rendered.
vulnerabilities.remediation:
1. Always sanitize and validate user input before rendering
2. Use framework-provided auto-escaping (enabled by default in most frameworks)
3. Avoid innerHTML, outerHTML, and document.write with user input
4. Use .textContent or .innerText instead of .innerHTML for text
5. Implement Content Security Policy (CSP) headers
6. Use DOMPurify or similar library if HTML rendering is required
7. Never use javascript:, data:, or vbscript: protocols with user input
8. Prefer safe framework methods: .text() in jQuery, default rendering in React

4. If shell features are needed, use shlex.quote() for argument escaping

vulnerabilities.title: Hardcoded Generic API Key
vulnerabilities.description: Generic API Key found: apiKey =...********. Hardcoded secrets pose a security risk.
vulnerabilities.remediation:
1. Immediately revoke this API key/token
2. Remove the hardcoded credential from the source code
3. Use environment variables or a secrets manager (e.g., HashiCorp Vault, AWS Secrets Manager)
4. Rotate all credentials that may have been exposed
5. Review access logs for unauthorized usage

vulnerabilities.title: XSS: DOM-Based (innerHTML Manipulation)
vulnerabilities.description: Detected dangerous DOM manipulation: '.innerHTML = name'. Direct manipulation of innerHTML, outerHTML, or document.write with unsanitized input can lead to XSS attacks. Attackers can inject malicious scripts that execute in the user's browser context, potentially stealing cookies, session tokens, or performing actions on behalf of the user. This is especially critical in MCP web UIs where user-provided tool descriptions or results might be rendered.
vulnerabilities.remediation:
1. Always sanitize and validate user input before rendering
2. Use framework-provided auto-escaping (enabled by default in most frameworks)
3. Avoid innerHTML, outerHTML, and document.write with user input
4. Use .textContent or .innerText instead of .innerHTML for text
5. Implement Content Security Policy (CSP) headers
6. Use DOMPurify or similar library if HTML rendering is required
7. Never use javascript:, data:, or vbscript: protocols with user input
8. Prefer safe framework methods: .text() in jQuery, default rendering in React

vulnerabilities.title: Hardcoded AWS Access Key
vulnerabilities.description: AWS Access Key found: AKIA1A2B...********. This grants programmatic access to AWS resources.
vulnerabilities.remediation:
1. Remove the hardcoded secret from source code
2. Use environment variables or a secrets manager
3. Rotate the exposed credential
4. Implement secret scanning in CI/CD pipeline

vulnerabilities.title: Command Injection via os.system()
vulnerabilities.description: Direct command execution using os.system() detected. This allows execution of arbitrary shell commands and can lead to remote code execution if user input is not properly sanitized.
vulnerabilities.remediation:
1. Avoid os.system() for executing commands
2. Use subprocess.run() with shell=False and list arguments
3. Validate and sanitize all user inputs
4. Use shlex.quote() for shell argument escaping if shell is necessary
5. Consider using safer alternatives like pathlib for file operations

vulnerabilities.title: Command Injection via subprocess.call(shell=True)
vulnerabilities.description: subprocess.call() with shell=True detected. Using shell=True passes commands through the shell, enabling command injection if user input is included.
vulnerabilities.remediation:
1. Use shell=False (default) with subprocess.call()
2. Pass command as a list of arguments instead of string
3. Example: subprocess.call(['ls', '-l']) instead of subprocess.call('ls -l', shell=True)
4. If shell features are needed, use shlex.quote() for argument escaping

3. Use environment variables or a secrets manager (e.g., HashiCorp Vault, AWS Secrets Manager)
4. Rotate all credentials that may have been exposed
5. Review access logs for unauthorized usage

vulnerabilities.title: XSS: DOM-Based (innerHTML Manipulation)
vulnerabilities.description: Detected dangerous DOM manipulation: '.innerHTML = name'. Direct manipulation of innerHTML, outerHTML, or document.write with unsanitized input can lead to XSS attacks. Attackers can inject malicious scripts that execute in the user's browser context, potentially stealing cookies, session tokens, or performing actions on behalf of the user. This is especially critical in MCP web UIs where user-provided tool descriptions or results might be rendered.
vulnerabilities.remediation:
1. Always sanitize and validate user input before rendering
2. Use framework-provided auto-escaping (enabled by default in most frameworks)
3. Avoid innerHTML, outerHTML, and document.write with user input
4. Use .textContent or .innerText instead of .innerHTML for text
5. Implement Content Security Policy (CSP) headers
6. Use DOMPurify or similar library if HTML rendering is required
7. Never use javascript:, data:, or vbscript: protocols with user input
8. Prefer safe framework methods: .text() in jQuery, default rendering in React

vulnerabilities.title: Hardcoded AWS Access Key
vulnerabilities.description: AWS Access Key found: AKIA1A2B...********. This grants programmatic access to AWS resources.
vulnerabilities.remediation:
1. Remove the hardcoded secret from source code
2. Use environment variables or a secrets manager
3. Rotate the exposed credential
4. Implement secret scanning in CI/CD pipeline

vulnerabilities.title: Command Injection via os.system()
vulnerabilities.description: Direct command execution using os.system() detected. This allows execution of arbitrary shell commands and can lead to remote code execution if user input is not properly sanitized.
vulnerabilities.remediation:
1. Avoid os.system() for executing commands
2. Use subprocess.run() with shell=False and list arguments
3. Validate and sanitize all user inputs
4. Use shlex.quote() for shell argument escaping if shell is necessary
5. Consider using safer alternatives like pathlib for file operations

vulnerabilities.title: Command Injection via subprocess.call(shell=True)
vulnerabilities.description: subprocess.call() with shell=True detected. Using shell=True passes commands through the shell, enabling command injection if user input is included.
vulnerabilities.remediation:
1. Use shell=False (default) with subprocess.call()
2. Pass command as a list of arguments instead of string
3. Example: subprocess.call(['ls', '-l']) instead of subprocess.call('ls -l', shell=True)
4. If shell features are needed, use shlex.quote() for argument escaping

vulnerabilities.title: Command Injection via os.system()
vulnerabilities.description: Direct command execution using os.system() detected. This allows execution of arbitrary shell commands and can lead to remote code execution if user input is not properly sanitized.
vulnerabilities.remediation:
1. Avoid os.system() for executing commands
2. Use subprocess.run() with shell=False and list arguments
3. Validate and sanitize all user inputs
4. Use shlex.quote() for shell argument escaping if shell is necessary
5. Consider using safer alternatives like pathlib for file operations

vulnerabilities.title: Command Injection via subprocess.call(shell=True)
vulnerabilities.description: subprocess.call() with shell=True detected. Using shell=True passes commands through the shell, enabling command injection if user input is included.
vulnerabilities.remediation:
1. Use shell=False (default) with subprocess.call()
2. Pass command as a list of arguments instead of string
3. Example: subprocess.call(['ls', '-l']) instead of subprocess.call('ls -l', shell=True)
4. If shell features are needed, use shlex.quote() for argument escaping

vulnerabilities.title: XSS: DOM-Based (innerHTML Manipulation)
vulnerabilities.description: Detected dangerous DOM manipulation: '.innerHTML = name'. Direct manipulation of innerHTML, outerHTML, or document.write with unsanitized input can lead to XSS attacks. Attackers can inject malicious scripts that execute in the user's browser context, potentially stealing cookies, session tokens, or performing actions on behalf of the user. This is especially critical in MCP web UIs where user-provided tool descriptions or results might be rendered.
vulnerabilities.remediation:
1. Always sanitize and validate user input before rendering
2. Use framework-provided auto-escaping (enabled by default in most frameworks)
3. Avoid innerHTML, outerHTML, and document.write with user input
4. Use .textContent or .innerText instead of .innerHTML for text
5. Implement Content Security Policy (CSP) headers
6. Use DOMPurify or similar library if HTML rendering is required
7. Never use javascript:, data:, or vbscript: protocols with user input
8. Prefer safe framework methods: .text() in jQuery, default rendering in React

vulnerabilities.remediation:
1. Remove the hardcoded secret from source code
2. Use environment variables or a secrets manager
3. Rotate the exposed credential
4. Implement secret scanning in CI/CD pipeline

vulnerabilities.title: Command Injection via os.system()
vulnerabilities.description: Direct command execution using os.system() detected. This allows execution of arbitrary shell commands and can lead to remote code execution if user input is not properly sanitized.
vulnerabilities.remediation:
1. Avoid os.system() for executing commands
2. Use subprocess.run() with shell=False and list arguments
3. Validate and sanitize all user inputs
4. Use shlex.quote() for shell argument escaping if shell is necessary
5. Consider using safer alternatives like pathlib for file operations

vulnerabilities.title: Command Injection via subprocess.call(shell=True)
vulnerabilities.description: subprocess.call() with shell=True detected. Using shell=True passes commands through the shell, enabling command injection if user input is included.
vulnerabilities.remediation:
1. Use shell=False (default) with subprocess.call()
2. Pass command as a list of arguments instead of string
3. Example: subprocess.call(['ls', '-l']) instead of subprocess.call('ls -l', shell=True)
4. If shell features are needed, use shlex.quote() for argument escaping

vulnerabilities.title: SQL Injection via Django raw()
vulnerabilities.description: Django User.objects.raw() used with f-string. This allows direct SQL injection if user input is included.
vulnerabilities.code_snippet: users = User.objects.raw(f"SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = {user_id}")
vulnerabilities.remediation:
1. Use Django ORM methods (filter, get) instead of raw SQL
2. If raw SQL is needed, use parameterized queries: raw('sql %s', [params])
3. NEVER use f-strings or format() to build SQL queries

vulnerabilities.title: XSS: Unescaped Template Rendering
vulnerabilities.description:
Detected unescaped template rendering: 'mark_safe(f"
{user_input}
")'. Using '|safe', 'mark_safe', or similar template filters disables automatic HTML escaping. This is dangerous when rendering user-provided content. Template engines like Jinja2 and Django automatically escape variables by default for security. Marking content as 'safe' removes this protection and can lead to stored XSS if the content comes from user input or untrusted sources.
vulnerabilities.remediation:
1. Always sanitize and validate user input before rendering
2. Use framework-provided auto-escaping (enabled by default in most frameworks)
3. Avoid innerHTML, outerHTML, and document.write with user input
4. Use .textContent or .innerText instead of .innerHTML for text
5. Implement Content Security Policy (CSP) headers
6. Use DOMPurify or similar library if HTML rendering is required
7. Never use javascript:, data:, or vbscript: protocols with user input
8. Prefer safe framework methods: .text() in jQuery, default rendering in React

vulnerabilities.title: Config: Debug Mode Enabled
vulnerabilities.description: Detected debug mode enabled: 'reload=True'. Running applications in debug mode in production exposes sensitive information including stack traces, environment variables, and internal application structure. Attackers can use this information to identify vulnerabilities and plan attacks. This is critical for MCP servers which may handle sensitive user data.
vulnerabilities.remediation:
1. Disable debug mode in production environments
2. Use environment variables to control debug settings
3. Set DEBUG=False or debug: false in production configs
4. Use separate configuration files for dev and production
5. Implement proper logging without exposing internals
6. Configure error pages that don't leak information
7. Review deployment checklist before production releases

vulnerabilities.title: Config: Weak Session/Secret Configuration
vulnerabilities.description: Detected weak secret configuration: 'config['SECRET_KEY'] = 'dev''. Weak or default secret keys allow attackers to forge sessions, decrypt data, and bypass authentication. Insecure cookie settings enable session theft.
vulnerabilities.remediation:
1. Use strong, randomly generated secret keys (32+ bytes)
2. Store secrets in environment variables, not code
3. Set SESSION_COOKIE_SECURE=True (HTTPS only)
4. Set SESSION_COOKIE_HTTPONLY=True (prevent XSS)
5. Set SESSION_COOKIE_SAMESITE='Lax' or 'Strict'
6. Rotate secrets regularly
7. Use secret management tools (Vault, AWS Secrets Manager)

vulnerabilities.title: XSS: DOM-Based (innerHTML Manipulation)
vulnerabilities.description: Detected dangerous DOM manipulation: '.innerHTML = name'. Direct manipulation of innerHTML, outerHTML, or document.write with unsanitized input can lead to XSS attacks. Attackers can inject malicious scripts that execute in the user's browser context, potentially stealing cookies, session tokens, or performing actions on behalf of the user. This is especially critical in MCP web UIs where user-provided tool descriptions or results might be rendered.
vulnerabilities.remediation:
1. Always sanitize and validate user input before rendering
2. Use framework-provided auto-escaping (enabled by default in most frameworks)
3. Avoid innerHTML, outerHTML, and document.write with user input
4. Use .textContent or .innerText instead of .innerHTML for text
5. Implement Content Security Policy (CSP) headers
6. Use DOMPurify or similar library if HTML rendering is required
7. Never use javascript:, data:, or vbscript: protocols with user input
8. Prefer safe framework methods: .text() in jQuery, default rendering in React

1. Use Django ORM methods (filter, get) instead of raw SQL
2. If raw SQL is needed, use parameterized queries: raw('sql %s', [params])
3. NEVER use f-strings or format() to build SQL queries

vulnerabilities.title: Config: Debug Mode Enabled
vulnerabilities.description: Detected debug mode enabled: 'reload=True'. Running applications in debug mode in production exposes sensitive information including stack traces, environment variables, and internal application structure. Attackers can use this information to identify vulnerabilities and plan attacks. This is critical for MCP servers which may handle sensitive user data.
vulnerabilities.remediation:
1. Disable debug mode in production environments
2. Use environment variables to control debug settings
3. Set DEBUG=False or debug: false in production configs
4. Use separate configuration files for dev and production
5. Implement proper logging without exposing internals
6. Configure error pages that don't leak information
7. Review deployment checklist before production releases

vulnerabilities.title: Config: Weak Session/Secret Configuration
vulnerabilities.description: Detected weak secret configuration: 'config['SECRET_KEY'] = 'dev''. Weak or default secret keys allow attackers to forge sessions, decrypt data, and bypass authentication. Insecure cookie settings enable session theft.
vulnerabilities.remediation:
1. Use strong, randomly generated secret keys (32+ bytes)
2. Store secrets in environment variables, not code
3. Set SESSION_COOKIE_SECURE=True (HTTPS only)
4. Set SESSION_COOKIE_HTTPONLY=True (prevent XSS)
5. Set SESSION_COOKIE_SAMESITE='Lax' or 'Strict'
6. Rotate secrets regularly
7. Use secret management tools (Vault, AWS Secrets Manager)

vulnerabilities.title: Hardcoded Anthropic API Key
vulnerabilities.description: Anthropic Claude API Key found: sk-ant-a...********. This grants access to Claude AI models.
vulnerabilities.remediation:
1. Immediately revoke this API key/token
2. Remove the hardcoded credential from the source code
3. Use environment variables or a secrets manager (e.g., HashiCorp Vault, AWS Secrets Manager)
4. Rotate all credentials that may have been exposed
5. Review access logs for unauthorized usage

vulnerabilities.title: Command Injection via os.system()
vulnerabilities.description: Direct command execution using os.system() detected. This allows execution of arbitrary shell commands and can lead to remote code execution if user input is not properly sanitized.
vulnerabilities.remediation:
1. Avoid os.system() for executing commands
2. Use subprocess.run() with shell=False and list arguments
3. Validate and sanitize all user inputs
4. Use shlex.quote() for shell argument escaping if shell is necessary
5. Consider using safer alternatives like pathlib for file operations

vulnerabilities.title: Command Injection via subprocess.call(shell=True)
vulnerabilities.description: subprocess.call() with shell=True detected. Using shell=True passes commands through the shell, enabling command injection if user input is included.
vulnerabilities.remediation:
1. Use shell=False (default) with subprocess.call()
2. Pass command as a list of arguments instead of string
3. Example: subprocess.call(['ls', '-l']) instead of subprocess.call('ls -l', shell=True)
4. If shell features are needed, use shlex.quote() for argument escaping

vulnerabilities.title: SQL Injection via Django raw()
vulnerabilities.description: Django User.objects.raw() used with f-string. This allows direct SQL injection if user input is included.
vulnerabilities.code_snippet: users = User.objects.raw(f"SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = {user_id}")
vulnerabilities.remediation:
1. Use Django ORM methods (filter, get) instead of raw SQL
2. If raw SQL is needed, use parameterized queries: raw('sql %s', [params])
3. NEVER use f-strings or format() to build SQL queries

vulnerabilities.title: Command Injection via os.system()
vulnerabilities.description: Direct command execution using os.system() detected. This allows execution of arbitrary shell commands and can lead to remote code execution if user input is not properly sanitized.
vulnerabilities.remediation:
1. Avoid os.system() for executing commands
2. Use subprocess.run() with shell=False and list arguments
3. Validate and sanitize all user inputs
4. Use shlex.quote() for shell argument escaping if shell is necessary
5. Consider using safer alternatives like pathlib for file operations

vulnerabilities.title: Command Injection via subprocess.call(shell=True)
vulnerabilities.description: subprocess.call() with shell=True detected. Using shell=True passes commands through the shell, enabling command injection if user input is included.
vulnerabilities.remediation:
1. Use shell=False (default) with subprocess.call()
2. Pass command as a list of arguments instead of string
3. Example: subprocess.call(['ls', '-l']) instead of subprocess.call('ls -l', shell=True)
4. If shell features are needed, use shlex.quote() for argument escaping

vulnerabilities.title: SQL Injection via Django raw()
vulnerabilities.description: Django User.objects.raw() used with f-string. This allows direct SQL injection if user input is included.
vulnerabilities.code_snippet: users = User.objects.raw(f"SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = {user_id}")
vulnerabilities.remediation:
1. Use Django ORM methods (filter, get) instead of raw SQL
2. If raw SQL is needed, use parameterized queries: raw('sql %s', [params])
3. NEVER use f-strings or format() to build SQL queries

vulnerabilities.title: Config: Debug Mode Enabled
vulnerabilities.description: Detected debug mode enabled: 'reload=True'. Running applications in debug mode in production exposes sensitive information including stack traces, environment variables, and internal application structure. Attackers can use this information to identify vulnerabilities and plan attacks. This is critical for MCP servers which may handle sensitive user data.
vulnerabilities.remediation:
1. Disable debug mode in production environments
2. Use environment variables to control debug settings
3. Set DEBUG=False or debug: false in production configs
4. Use separate configuration files for dev and production
5. Implement proper logging without exposing internals
6. Configure error pages that don't leak information
7. Review deployment checklist before production releases

vulnerabilities.title: Config: Weak Session/Secret Configuration
vulnerabilities.description: Detected weak secret configuration: 'config['SECRET_KEY'] = 'dev''. Weak or default secret keys allow attackers to forge sessions, decrypt data, and bypass authentication. Insecure cookie settings enable session theft.
vulnerabilities.remediation:
1. Use strong, randomly generated secret keys (32+ bytes)
2. Store secrets in environment variables, not code
3. Set SESSION_COOKIE_SECURE=True (HTTPS only)
4. Set SESSION_COOKIE_HTTPONLY=True (prevent XSS)
5. Set SESSION_COOKIE_SAMESITE='Lax' or 'Strict'
6. Rotate secrets regularly
7. Use secret management tools (Vault, AWS Secrets Manager)

vulnerabilities.title: Config: Debug Mode Enabled
vulnerabilities.description: Detected debug mode enabled: 'debug=True'. 3. Validate and sanitize all user inputs
4. Use shlex.quote() for shell argument escaping if shell is necessary
5. Consider using safer alternatives like pathlib for file operations

3. Use environment variables or a secrets manager (e.g., HashiCorp Vault, AWS Secrets Manager)

vulnerabilities.title: Command Injection via os.system()
vulnerabilities.description: Direct command execution using os.system() detected. This allows execution of arbitrary shell commands and can lead to remote code execution if user input is not properly sanitized.
vulnerabilities.remediation:
1. Avoid os.system() for executing commands
2. Use subprocess.run() with shell=False and list arguments
3. Validate and sanitize all user inputs
4. Use shlex.quote() for shell argument escaping if shell is necessary
5. Consider using safer alternatives like pathlib for file operations

vulnerabilities.title: SQL Injection via Django raw()
vulnerabilities.description: Django User.objects.raw() used with f-string. This allows direct SQL injection if user input is included.
vulnerabilities.code_snippet: users = User.objects.raw(f"SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = {user_id}")
vulnerabilities.remediation:
1. Use Django ORM methods (filter, get) instead of raw SQL
2. If raw SQL is needed, use parameterized queries: raw('sql %s', [params])
3. NEVER use f-strings or format() to build SQL queries

vulnerabilities.title: Tool Poisoning: Suspicious Tool Name Pattern
vulnerabilities.description: Detected suspicious tool name: '"__main__"'. Full-schema poisoning embeds malicious directives in tool names, not just descriptions. Tool names like 'always_run_first', 'override_*', or '__instruction__' signal an attempt to manipulate agent tool selection or execution order. This is the 'Full-Schema Poisoning' (FSP) technique documented in 2025 MCP security research.
vulnerabilities.remediation:
1. Review and validate all tool descriptions and metadata
2. Implement strict input validation for tool configurations
3. Use allowlists for acceptable instruction patterns
4. Add human review process for tool registration
5. Implement runtime monitoring for unexpected AI behaviors
6. Use structured formats (JSON schema) to prevent free-form manipulation

vulnerabilities.title: Config: Debug Mode Enabled
vulnerabilities.description: Detected debug mode enabled: 'reload=True'. Running applications in debug mode in production exposes sensitive information including stack traces, environment variables, and internal application structure. Attackers can use this information to identify vulnerabilities and plan attacks. This is critical for MCP servers which may handle sensitive user data.
vulnerabilities.remediation:
1. Disable debug mode in production environments
2. Use environment variables to control debug settings
3. Set DEBUG=False or debug: false in production configs
4. Use separate configuration files for dev and production
5. Implement proper logging without exposing internals
6. Configure error pages that don't leak information
7. Review deployment checklist before production releases

vulnerabilities.title: Tool Poisoning: Suspicious Tool Name Pattern

vulnerabilities.title: Config: Weak Session/Secret Configuration
vulnerabilities.description: Detected weak secret configuration: 'config['SECRET_KEY'] = 'dev''. Weak or default secret keys allow attackers to forge sessions, decrypt data, and bypass authentication. Insecure cookie settings enable session theft.
vulnerabilities.remediation:
1. Use strong, randomly generated secret keys (32+ bytes)
2. Store secrets in environment variables, not code
3. Set SESSION_COOKIE_SECURE=True (HTTPS only)
4. Set SESSION_COOKIE_HTTPONLY=True (prevent XSS)
5. Set SESSION_COOKIE_SAMESITE='Lax' or 'Strict'
6. Rotate secrets regularly
7. Use secret management tools (Vault, AWS Secrets Manager)

vulnerabilities.title: Command Injection via subprocess.call(shell=True)
vulnerabilities.description: subprocess.call() with shell=True detected. Using shell=True passes commands through the shell, enabling command injection if user input is included.
vulnerabilities.remediation:
1. Use shell=False (default) with subprocess.call()
2. Pass command as a list of arguments instead of string
3. Example: subprocess.call(['ls', '-l']) instead of subprocess.call('ls -l', shell=True)
4. If shell features are needed, use shlex.quote() for argument escaping

vulnerabilities.remediation:
1. Review and validate all tool descriptions and metadata
2. Implement strict input validation for tool configurations
3. Use allowlists for acceptable instruction patterns
4. Add human review process for tool registration
5. Implement runtime monitoring for unexpected AI behaviors
6. Use structured formats (JSON schema) to prevent free-form manipulation

vulnerabilities.title: Config: Debug Mode Enabled
vulnerabilities.description: Detected debug mode enabled: 'reload=True'. Running applications in debug mode in production exposes sensitive information including stack traces, environment variables, and internal application structure. Attackers can use this information to identify vulnerabilities and plan attacks. This is critical for MCP servers which may handle sensitive user data.
vulnerabilities.remediation:
1. Disable debug mode in production environments
2. Use environment variables to control debug settings
3. Set DEBUG=False or debug: false in production configs
4. Use separate configuration files for dev and production
5. Implement proper logging without exposing internals
6. Configure error pages that don't leak information
7. Review deployment checklist before production releases

vulnerabilities.title: Tool Poisoning: Suspicious Tool Name Pattern
vulnerabilities.description: Detected suspicious tool name: '"__main__"'. Full-schema poisoning embeds malicious directives in tool names, not just descriptions. Tool names like 'always_run_first', 'override_*', or '__instruction__' signal an attempt to manipulate agent tool selection or execution order. This is the 'Full-Schema Poisoning' (FSP) technique documented in 2025 MCP security research.

vulnerabilities.title: Config: Weak Session/Secret Configuration
vulnerabilities.description: Detected weak secret configuration: 'config['SECRET_KEY'] = 'dev''. Weak or default secret keys allow attackers to forge sessions, decrypt data, and bypass authentication. Insecure cookie settings enable session theft.
vulnerabilities.remediation:
1. Use strong, randomly generated secret keys (32+ bytes)
2. Store secrets in environment variables, not code
3. Set SESSION_COOKIE_SECURE=True (HTTPS only)
4. Set SESSION_COOKIE_HTTPONLY=True (prevent XSS)
5. Set SESSION_COOKIE_SAMESITE='Lax' or 'Strict'
6. Rotate secrets regularly
7. Use secret management tools (Vault, AWS Secrets Manager)

vulnerabilities.title: Command Injection via os.system()
vulnerabilities.description: Direct command execution using os.system() detected. This allows execution of arbitrary shell commands and can lead to remote code execution if user input is not properly sanitized.
vulnerabilities.remediation:
1. Avoid os.system() for executing commands
2. Use subprocess.run() with shell=False and list arguments
3. Validate and sanitize all user inputs
4. Use shlex.quote() for shell argument escaping if shell is necessary
5. Consider using safer alternatives like pathlib for file operations

vulnerabilities.title: Command Injection via subprocess.call(shell=True)
vulnerabilities.description: subprocess.call() with shell=True detected. Using shell=True passes commands through the shell, enabling command injection if user input is included.
vulnerabilities.remediation:
1. Use shell=False (default) with subprocess.call()
2. Pass command as a list of arguments instead of string
3. Example: subprocess.call(['ls', '-l']) instead of subprocess.call('ls -l', shell=True)
4. If shell features are needed, use shlex.quote() for argument escaping

vulnerabilities.title: SQL Injection via Django raw()
vulnerabilities.description: Django User.objects.raw() used with f-string. This allows direct SQL injection if user input is included.
vulnerabilities.code_snippet: users = User.objects.raw(f"SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = {user_id}")
vulnerabilities.remediation:
1. Use Django ORM methods (filter, get) instead of raw SQL
2. If raw SQL is needed, use parameterized queries: raw('sql %s', [params])
3. NEVER use f-strings or format() to build SQL queries

vulnerabilities.title: Command Injection via os.system()
vulnerabilities.description: Direct command execution using os.system() detected. This allows execution of arbitrary shell commands and can lead to remote code execution if user input is not properly sanitized.
vulnerabilities.remediation:
1. Avoid os.system() for executing commands
2. Use subprocess.run() with shell=False and list arguments
3. Validate and sanitize all user inputs
4. Use shlex.quote() for shell argument escaping if shell is necessary
5. Consider using safer alternatives like pathlib for file operations

vulnerabilities.title: Command Injection via subprocess.call(shell=True)
vulnerabilities.description: subprocess.call() with shell=True detected. Using shell=True passes commands through the shell, enabling command injection if user input is included.
vulnerabilities.remediation:
1. Use shell=False (default) with subprocess.call()
2. Pass command as a list of arguments instead of string
3. Example: subprocess.call(['ls', '-l']) instead of subprocess.call('ls -l', shell=True)
4. If shell features are needed, use shlex.quote() for argument escaping

vulnerabilities.title: SQL Injection via Django raw()
vulnerabilities.description: Django User.objects.raw() used with f-string. This allows direct SQL injection if user input is included.
vulnerabilities.code_snippet: users = User.objects.raw(f"SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = {user_id}")
vulnerabilities.remediation:
1. Use Django ORM methods (filter, get) instead of raw SQL
2. If raw SQL is needed, use parameterized queries: raw('sql %s', [params])
3. NEVER use f-strings or format() to build SQL queries

vulnerabilities.title: Tool Poisoning: Suspicious Tool Name Pattern
vulnerabilities.description: Detected suspicious tool name: '"__main__"'. Full-schema poisoning embeds malicious directives in tool names, not just descriptions. Tool names like 'always_run_first', 'override_*', or '__instruction__' signal an attempt to manipulate agent tool selection or execution order. This is the 'Full-Schema Poisoning' (FSP) technique documented in 2025 MCP security research.
vulnerabilities.remediation:
1. Review and validate all tool descriptions and metadata
2. Implement strict input validation for tool configurations
3. Use allowlists for acceptable instruction patterns
4. Add human review process for tool registration
5. Implement runtime monitoring for unexpected AI behaviors
6. Use structured formats (JSON schema) to prevent free-form manipulation

vulnerabilities.title: Config: Debug Mode Enabled
vulnerabilities.description: Detected debug mode enabled: 'reload=True'. Running applications in debug mode in production exposes sensitive information including stack traces, environment variables, and internal application structure. Attackers can use this information to identify vulnerabilities and plan attacks. This is critical for MCP servers which may handle sensitive user data.
vulnerabilities.remediation:
1. Disable debug mode in production environments
2. Use environment variables to control debug settings
3. Set DEBUG=False or debug: false in production configs
4. Use separate configuration files for dev and production
5. Implement proper logging without exposing internals
6. Configure error pages that don't leak information
7. Review deployment checklist before production releases

vulnerabilities.title: Config: Weak Session/Secret Configuration
vulnerabilities.description: Detected weak secret configuration: 'config['SECRET_KEY'] = 'dev''. Weak or default secret keys allow attackers to forge sessions, decrypt data, and bypass authentication. Insecure cookie settings enable session theft.
vulnerabilities.remediation:
1. Use strong, randomly generated secret keys (32+ bytes)
2. Store secrets in environment variables, not code
3. Set SESSION_COOKIE_SECURE=True (HTTPS only)
4. Set SESSION_COOKIE_HTTPONLY=True (prevent XSS)
5. Set SESSION_COOKIE_SAMESITE='Lax' or 'Strict'
6. Rotate secrets regularly
7. Use secret management tools (Vault, AWS Secrets Manager)

vulnerabilities.title: Config: Debug Mode Enabled
vulnerabilities.description: Detected debug mode enabled: 'debug=True'. Running applications in debug mode in production exposes sensitive information including stack traces, environment variables, and internal application structure. Attackers can use this information to identify vulnerabilities and plan attacks. This is critical for MCP servers which may handle sensitive user data.
vulnerabilities.remediation:
1. Disable debug mode in production environments
2. Use environment variables to control debug settings
3. Set DEBUG=False or debug: false in production configs
4. Use separate configuration files for dev and production
5. Implement proper logging without exposing internals
6. Configure error pages that don't leak information
7. Review deployment checklist before production releases

vulnerabilities.description: Direct command execution using os.system() detected. This allows execution of arbitrary shell commands and can lead to remote code execution if user input is not properly sanitized.
vulnerabilities.remediation:
1. Avoid os.system() for executing commands
2. Use subprocess.run() with shell=False and list arguments
3. Validate and sanitize all user inputs
4. Use shlex.quote() for shell argument escaping if shell is necessary
5. Consider using safer alternatives like pathlib for file operations

vulnerabilities.title: Command Injection via subprocess.call(shell=True)
vulnerabilities.description: subprocess.call() with shell=True detected. Using shell=True passes commands through the shell, enabling command injection if user input is included.
vulnerabilities.remediation:
1. Use shell=False (default) with subprocess.call()
2. Pass command as a list of arguments instead of string
3. Example: subprocess.call(['ls', '-l']) instead of subprocess.call('ls -l', shell=True)
4. If shell features are needed, use shlex.quote() for argument escaping

vulnerabilities.title: Hardcoded AWS Access Key
vulnerabilities.description: AWS Access Key found: AKIA1A2B...********. This grants programmatic access to AWS resources.
vulnerabilities.remediation:
1. Remove the hardcoded secret from source code
2. Use environment variables or a secrets manager
3. Rotate the exposed credential
4. Implement secret scanning in CI/CD pipeline

vulnerabilities.title: SQL Injection via Django raw()
vulnerabilities.description: Django User.objects.raw() used with f-string. This allows direct SQL injection if user input is included.
vulnerabilities.code_snippet: users = User.objects.raw(f"SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = {user_id}")
vulnerabilities.remediation:
1. Use Django ORM methods (filter, get) instead of raw SQL
2. If raw SQL is needed, use parameterized queries: raw('sql %s', [params])
3. NEVER use f-strings or format() to build SQL queries

vulnerabilities.title: Tool Poisoning: Suspicious Tool Name Pattern
vulnerabilities.description: Detected suspicious tool name: '"__main__"'. Full-schema poisoning embeds malicious directives in tool names, not just descriptions. Tool names like 'always_run_first', 'override_*', or '__instruction__' signal an attempt to manipulate agent tool selection or execution order. This is the 'Full-Schema Poisoning' (FSP) technique documented in 2025 MCP security research.
vulnerabilities.remediation:
1. Review and validate all tool descriptions and metadata
2. Implement strict input validation for tool configurations
3. Use allowlists for acceptable instruction patterns
4. Add human review process for tool registration
5. Implement runtime monitoring for unexpected AI behaviors
6. Use structured formats (JSON schema) to prevent free-form manipulation

vulnerabilities.title: Config: Debug Mode Enabled
vulnerabilities.description: Detected debug mode enabled: 'reload=True'. Running applications in debug mode in production exposes sensitive information including stack traces, environment variables, and internal application structure. Attackers can use this information to identify vulnerabilities and plan attacks. This is critical for MCP servers which may handle sensitive user data.
vulnerabilities.remediation:
1. Disable debug mode in production environments
2. Use environment variables to control debug settings
3. Set DEBUG=False or debug: false in production configs
4. Use separate configuration files for dev and production
5. Implement proper logging without exposing internals
6. Configure error pages that don't leak information
7. Review deployment checklist before production releases

vulnerabilities.title: Tool Poisoning: Suspicious Tool Name Pattern
vulnerabilities.description: Detected suspicious tool name: '"__main__"'. Full-schema poisoning embeds malicious directives in tool names, not just descriptions. Tool names like 'always_run_first', 'override_*', or '__instruction__' signal an attempt to manipulate agent tool selection or execution order. vulnerabilities.title: Config: Weak Session/Secret Configuration
vulnerabilities.description: Detected weak secret configuration: 'config['SECRET_KEY'] = 'dev''. Weak or default secret keys allow attackers to forge sessions, decrypt data, and bypass authentication. Insecure cookie settings enable session theft.
vulnerabilities.remediation:
1. Use strong, randomly generated secret keys (32+ bytes)
2. Store secrets in environment variables, not code
3. Set SESSION_COOKIE_SECURE=True (HTTPS only)
4. Set SESSION_COOKIE_HTTPONLY=True (prevent XSS)
5. Set SESSION_COOKIE_SAMESITE='Lax' or 'Strict'
6. Rotate secrets regularly
7. Use secret management tools (Vault, AWS Secrets Manager)

vulnerabilities.remediation:
1. Disable debug mode in production environments
2. Use environment variables to control debug settings
3. Set DEBUG=False or debug: false in production configs
4. Use separate configuration files for dev and production
5. Implement proper logging without exposing internals
6. Configure error pages that don't leak information
7. Review deployment checklist before production releases

Tool names like 'always_run_first', 'override_*', or '__instruction__' signal an attempt to manipulate agent tool selection or execution order. This is the 'Full-Schema Poisoning' (FSP) technique documented in 2025 MCP security research.
vulnerabilities.remediation:
1. Review and validate all tool descriptions and metadata
2. Implement strict input validation for tool configurations
3. Use allowlists for acceptable instruction patterns
4. Add human review process for tool registration
5. Implement runtime monitoring for unexpected AI behaviors
6. Use structured formats (JSON schema) to prevent free-form manipulation

vulnerabilities.title: Config: Weak Session/Secret Configuration
vulnerabilities.description: Detected weak secret configuration: 'config['SECRET_KEY'] = 'dev''. Weak or default secret keys allow attackers to forge sessions, decrypt data, and bypass authentication. Insecure cookie settings enable session theft.
vulnerabilities.remediation:
1. Use strong, randomly generated secret keys (32+ bytes)
2. Store secrets in environment variables, not code
3. Set SESSION_COOKIE_SECURE=True (HTTPS only)
4. Set SESSION_COOKIE_HTTPONLY=True (prevent XSS)
5. Set SESSION_COOKIE_SAMESITE='Lax' or 'Strict'
6. Rotate secrets regularly
7. Use secret management tools (Vault, AWS Secrets Manager)

vulnerabilities.title: Config: Debug Mode Enabled
vulnerabilities.description: Detected debug mode enabled: 'debug=True'. Running applications in debug mode in production exposes sensitive information including stack traces, environment variables, and internal application structure. Attackers can use this information to identify vulnerabilities and plan attacks. This is critical for MCP servers which may handle sensitive user data.
vulnerabilities.remediation:
1. Disable debug mode in production environments
2. Use environment variables to control debug settings
3. Set DEBUG=False or debug: false in production configs
4. Use separate configuration files for dev and production
5. Implement proper logging without exposing internals
6. Configure error pages that don't leak information
7. Review deployment checklist before production releases

vulnerabilities.title: Config: Debug Mode Enabled
vulnerabilities.description: Detected debug mode enabled: 'app.run(debug=True'. Running applications in debug mode in production exposes sensitive information including stack traces, environment variables, and internal application structure. Attackers can use this information to identify vulnerabilities and plan attacks. This is critical for MCP servers which may handle sensitive user data.
vulnerabilities.remediation:
1. Disable debug mode in production environments
2. Use environment variables to control debug settings
3. Set DEBUG=False or debug: false in production configs
4. Use separate configuration files for dev and production
5. Implement proper logging without exposing internals
6. Configure error pages that don't leak information
7. Review deployment checklist before production releases

vulnerabilities.title: Hardcoded AWS Access Key
vulnerabilities.description: AWS Access Key found: AKIA1A2B...********. This grants programmatic access to AWS resources.
vulnerabilities.remediation:
1. Remove the hardcoded secret from source code
2. Use environment variables or a secrets manager
3. Rotate the exposed credential
4. Implement secret scanning in CI/CD pipeline

vulnerabilities.title: Hardcoded OpenAI API Key
vulnerabilities.description: OpenAI API Key found: sk-12345...********. This provides access to OpenAI's API services.
vulnerabilities.remediation:
1. Immediately revoke this API key/token
2. Remove the hardcoded credential from the source code
3. Use environment variables or a secrets manager (e.g., HashiCorp Vault, AWS Secrets Manager)
4. Rotate all credentials that may have been exposed
5. Review access logs for unauthorized usage

vulnerabilities.title: Hardcoded Generic API Key
vulnerabilities.description: Generic API Key found: API_KEY ...********. Hardcoded secrets pose a security risk.
vulnerabilities.remediation:
1. Immediately revoke this API key/token
2. Remove the hardcoded credential from the source code
3. Use environment variables or a secrets manager (e.g., HashiCorp Vault, AWS Secrets Manager)
4. Rotate all credentials that may have been exposed
5. Review access logs for unauthorized usage

vulnerabilities.title: Command Injection via os.system()
vulnerabilities.description: Direct command execution using os.system() detected. This allows execution of arbitrary shell commands and can lead to remote code execution if user input is not properly sanitized.
vulnerabilities.remediation:
1. Avoid os.system() for executing commands
2. Use subprocess.run() with shell=False and list arguments
3. Validate and sanitize all user inputs
4. Use shlex.quote() for shell argument escaping if shell is necessary
5. Consider using safer alternatives like pathlib for file operations

vulnerabilities.title: Command Injection via subprocess.call(shell=True)
vulnerabilities.description: subprocess.call() with shell=True detected. Using shell=True passes commands through the shell, enabling command injection if user input is included.
vulnerabilities.remediation:
1. Use shell=False (default) with subprocess.call()
2. Pass command as a list of arguments instead of string
3. Example: subprocess.call(['ls', '-l']) instead of subprocess.call('ls -l', shell=True)
4. If shell features are needed, use shlex.quote() for argument escaping

vulnerabilities.title: SQL Injection via Django raw()
vulnerabilities.description: Django User.objects.raw() used with f-string. This allows direct SQL injection if user input is included.
vulnerabilities.code_snippet: users = User.objects.raw(f"SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = {user_id}")
vulnerabilities.remediation:
1. Use Django ORM methods (filter, get) instead of raw SQL
2. If raw SQL is needed, use parameterized queries: raw('sql %s', [params])
3. NEVER use f-strings or format() to build SQL queries

3. Validate and sanitize all user inputs
4. Use shlex.quote() for shell argument escaping if shell is necessary
5. Consider using safer alternatives like pathlib for file operations

vulnerabilities.title: Command Injection via subprocess.call(shell=True)
vulnerabilities.description: subprocess.call() with shell=True detected. Using shell=True passes commands through the shell, enabling command injection if user input is included.
vulnerabilities.remediation:
1. Use shell=False (default) with subprocess.call()
2. Pass command as a list of arguments instead of string
3. Example: subprocess.call(['ls', '-l']) instead of subprocess.call('ls -l', shell=True)
4. If shell features are needed, use shlex.quote() for argument escaping

vulnerabilities.title: SQL Injection via Django raw()
vulnerabilities.description: Django User.objects.raw() used with f-string. This allows direct SQL injection if user input is included.
vulnerabilities.code_snippet: users = User.objects.raw(f"SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = {user_id}")
vulnerabilities.remediation:
1. Use Django ORM methods (filter, get) instead of raw SQL
2. If raw SQL is needed, use parameterized queries: raw('sql %s', [params])
3. NEVER use f-strings or format() to build SQL queries

vulnerabilities.title: Tool Poisoning: Suspicious Tool Name Pattern
vulnerabilities.description: Detected suspicious tool name: '"__main__"'. Full-schema poisoning embeds malicious directives in tool names, not just descriptions. Tool names like 'always_run_first', 'override_*', or '__instruction__' signal an attempt to manipulate agent tool selection or execution order. This is the 'Full-Schema Poisoning' (FSP) technique documented in 2025 MCP security research.
vulnerabilities.remediation:
1. Review and validate all tool descriptions and metadata
2. Implement strict input validation for tool configurations
3. Use allowlists for acceptable instruction patterns
4. Add human review process for tool registration
5. Implement runtime monitoring for unexpected AI behaviors
6. Use structured formats (JSON schema) to prevent free-form manipulation

vulnerabilities.title: Config: Debug Mode Enabled
vulnerabilities.description: Detected debug mode enabled: 'reload=True'. Running applications in debug mode in production exposes sensitive information including stack traces, environment variables, and internal application structure. Attackers can use this information to identify vulnerabilities and plan attacks. This is critical for MCP servers which may handle sensitive user data.
vulnerabilities.remediation:
1. Disable debug mode in production environments
2. Use environment variables to control debug settings
3. Set DEBUG=False or debug: false in production configs
4. Use separate configuration files for dev and production
5. Implement proper logging without exposing internals
6. Configure error pages that don't leak information
7. Review deployment checklist before production releases

vulnerabilities.title: Config: Weak Session/Secret Configuration
vulnerabilities.description: Detected weak secret configuration: 'config['SECRET_KEY'] = 'dev''. Weak or default secret keys allow attackers to forge sessions, decrypt data, and bypass authentication. Insecure cookie settings enable session theft.
vulnerabilities.remediation:
1. Use strong, randomly generated secret keys (32+ bytes)
2. Store secrets in environment variables, not code
3. Set SESSION_COOKIE_SECURE=True (HTTPS only)
4. Set SESSION_COOKIE_HTTPONLY=True (prevent XSS)
5. Set SESSION_COOKIE_SAMESITE='Lax' or 'Strict'
6. Rotate secrets regularly
7. Use secret management tools (Vault, AWS Secrets Manager)

vulnerabilities.title: Command Injection via os.system()
vulnerabilities.description: Direct command execution using os.system() detected. This allows execution of arbitrary shell commands and can lead to remote code execution if user input is not properly sanitized.
vulnerabilities.remediation:
1. Avoid os.system() for executing commands
2. Use subprocess.run() with shell=False and list arguments
3. Validate and sanitize all user inputs
4. Use shlex.quote() for shell argument escaping if shell is necessary
5. Consider using safer alternatives like pathlib for file operations

vulnerabilities.title: SQL Injection via Django raw()
vulnerabilities.description: Django User.objects.raw() used with f-string. This allows direct SQL injection if user input is included.
vulnerabilities.code_snippet: users = User.objects.raw(f"SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = {user_id}")
vulnerabilities.remediation:
1. Use Django ORM methods (filter, get) instead of raw SQL
2. If raw SQL is needed, use parameterized queries: raw('sql %s', [params])
3. NEVER use f-strings or format() to build SQL queries

vulnerabilities.title: Tool Poisoning: Suspicious Tool Name Pattern
vulnerabilities.description: Detected suspicious tool name: '"__main__"'. Full-schema poisoning embeds malicious directives in tool names, not just descriptions. Tool names like 'always_run_first', 'override_*', or '__instruction__' signal an attempt to manipulate agent tool selection or execution order. This is the 'Full-Schema Poisoning' (FSP) technique documented in 2025 MCP security research.
vulnerabilities.remediation:
1. Review and validate all tool descriptions and metadata
2. Implement strict input validation for tool configurations
3. Use allowlists for acceptable instruction patterns
4. Add human review process for tool registration
5. Implement runtime monitoring for unexpected AI behaviors
6. Use structured formats (JSON schema) to prevent free-form manipulation

vulnerabilities.title: Config: Debug Mode Enabled
vulnerabilities.description: Detected debug mode enabled: 'reload=True'. Running applications in debug mode in production exposes sensitive information including stack traces, environment variables, and internal application structure. Attackers can use this information to identify vulnerabilities and plan attacks. This is critical for MCP servers which may handle sensitive user data.
vulnerabilities.remediation:
1. Disable debug mode in production environments
2. Use environment variables to control debug settings
3. Set DEBUG=False or debug: false in production configs
4. Use separate configuration files for dev and production
5. Implement proper logging without exposing internals
6. Configure error pages that don't leak information
7. Review deployment checklist before production releases

vulnerabilities.title: Tool Poisoning: Suspicious Tool Name Pattern
vulnerabilities.description: Detected suspicious tool name: '"__main__"'. Full-schema poisoning embeds malicious directives in tool names, not just descriptions. Tool names like 'always_run_first', 'override_*', or '__instruction__' signal an attempt to manipulate agent tool selection or execution order. 6. Use structured formats (JSON schema) to prevent free-form manipulation

vulnerabilities.title: Config: Weak Session/Secret Configuration
vulnerabilities.description: Detected weak secret configuration: 'config['SECRET_KEY'] = 'dev''. Weak or default secret keys allow attackers to forge sessions, decrypt data, and bypass authentication. Insecure cookie settings enable session theft.
vulnerabilities.remediation:
1. Use strong, randomly generated secret keys (32+ bytes)
2. Store secrets in environment variables, not code
3. Set SESSION_COOKIE_SECURE=True (HTTPS only)
4. Set SESSION_COOKIE_HTTPONLY=True (prevent XSS)
5. Set SESSION_COOKIE_SAMESITE='Lax' or 'Strict'
6. Rotate secrets regularly
7. Use secret management tools (Vault, AWS Secrets Manager)

vulnerabilities.title: Config: Debug Mode Enabled
vulnerabilities.description: Detected debug mode enabled: 'debug=True'. Running applications in debug mode in production exposes sensitive information including stack traces, environment variables, and internal application structure. vulnerabilities.remediation:
1. Disable debug mode in production environments
2. Use environment variables to control debug settings
3. Set DEBUG=False or debug: false in production configs
4. Use separate configuration files for dev and production
5. Implement proper logging without exposing internals
6. Configure error pages that don't leak information
7. Review deployment checklist before production releases
